questions.questionText: Metabolic syndrome is best defined as:
options: A single disease caused only by obesity
A clustering of metabolic abnormalities increasing risk of cardiovascular disease and diabetes
A genetic disorder affecting only HDL metabolism
A syndrome diagnosed only by fasting glucose
questions.explanation: Metabolic syndrome is a cluster of conditions, including obesity, dyslipidemia, hypertension, and hyperglycemia, associated with increased risk of cardiovascular disease and diabetes.

questions.questionText: According to the file, metabolic syndrome requires at least:
questions.explanation: The file states that metabolic syndrome is diagnosed when at least three of five specified conditions are present.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is one of the five main components of metabolic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Elevated fasting plasma glucose is one of the diagnostic components of metabolic syndrome.

questions.questionText: Which lipid abnormality is part of metabolic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Low HDL cholesterol is one of the criteria of metabolic syndrome.

questions.questionText: High serum triglycerides in metabolic syndrome mainly reflect abnormality in:
questions.explanation: Elevated triglycerides are a lipid abnormality and are included among the metabolic syndrome criteria.

questions.questionText: The main body-shape sign of metabolic syndrome is:
questions.explanation: The file describes central obesity, also called visceral, male-pattern, or apple-shaped adiposity, as the main sign.

questions.questionText: Central obesity refers mainly to fat accumulation around the:
questions.explanation: Central obesity means adipose tissue accumulation particularly around the waist and trunk.

questions.questionText: Metabolic syndrome is associated with increased risk of:
questions.explanation: The file states that metabolic syndrome is associated with risk of cardiovascular disease and diabetes.

questions.questionText: Which blood pressure abnormality is included in metabolic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Elevated blood pressure is one of the five diagnostic components of metabolic syndrome.

questions.questionText: Which glucose abnormality is included in metabolic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Raised fasting plasma glucose is a diagnostic criterion of metabolic syndrome.

questions.questionText: The syndrome is thought to involve an underlying disorder of:
questions.explanation: The file describes metabolic syndrome as related to abnormal energy utilization and storage.

questions.questionText: Most patients with metabolic syndrome are described as:
options: Older, obese, sedentary, and insulin resistant
Only children with no insulin resistance
questions.explanation: The file states most patients are older, obese, sedentary, and have some degree of insulin resistance.

questions.questionText: Which of the following may contribute to metabolic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Sedentary behavior or low physical activity is listed as an important factor.

questions.questionText: Acanthosis nigricans may be associated with:
questions.explanation: The file lists acanthosis nigricans among associated conditions.

questions.questionText: Fatty liver associated with obesity may progress to:
questions.explanation: Fatty liver, especially with concurrent obesity, may progress to nonalcoholic fatty liver disease.

questions.questionText: Which group contains only diagnostic features of metabolic syndrome?
options: Central obesity, high triglycerides, low HDL
Low glucose, high HDL, low BMI
Low LDL, high albumin, low BP
questions.explanation: Central obesity, elevated triglycerides, and reduced HDL are components of metabolic syndrome.

questions.questionText: According to the IDF criteria in the file, metabolic syndrome is diagnosed if how many listed criteria are true?
questions.explanation: The file states diagnosis is made if three of the listed criteria are present.

questions.questionText: According to the file, BMI criterion for metabolic syndrome is:
questions.explanation: The IDF definition listed in the file includes BMI >30 as one of the criteria.

questions.questionText: Raised triglycerides are defined as:
questions.explanation: Raised triglycerides are defined as >150 mg/dL, or treatment for this lipid abnormality.

questions.questionText: Reduced HDL cholesterol in males is defined as:
questions.explanation: In males, reduced HDL is defined as <40 mg/dL.

questions.questionText: Reduced HDL cholesterol in females is defined as:
questions.explanation: In females, reduced HDL is defined as <50 mg/dL.

questions.questionText: Raised blood pressure criterion is:
options: Systolic BP >130 or diastolic BP >85 mmHg
questions.explanation: Raised BP is systolic >130 or diastolic >85 mmHg, or treatment of previously diagnosed hypertension.

questions.questionText: Raised fasting plasma glucose criterion is:
questions.explanation: The file lists fasting plasma glucose >100 mg/dL as a criterion.

questions.questionText: Specific treatment for triglyceride abnormality:
questions.explanation: Raised triglycerides criterion includes either TG >150 mg/dL or specific treatment for this lipid abnormality.

questions.questionText: Treatment of previously diagnosed hypertension:
options: Counts toward the blood pressure criterion
questions.explanation: The blood pressure criterion includes raised BP or treatment of previously diagnosed hypertension.

questions.questionText: Previously diagnosed type 2 diabetes:
options: Counts toward the fasting glucose criterion
questions.explanation: Raised fasting plasma glucose criterion includes FPG >100 mg/dL or previously diagnosed type 2 diabetes.

questions.questionText: Which associated condition is more specifically listed in women?
questions.explanation: PCOS is listed as an associated condition in women.

questions.questionText: Which associated condition is specifically listed in men?
questions.explanation: Erectile dysfunction is listed as an associated condition in men.

questions.questionText: Which factor is listed as an important contributor to metabolic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Disrupted chronobiology or sleep is listed among important contributing factors.

questions.questionText: Which dietary factor is specifically mentioned as important?
questions.explanation: Diet, particularly sugar-sweetened beverage consumption, is mentioned as an important factor.

questions.questionText: Which statement about the pathophysiology of metabolic syndrome is most accurate according to the file?
options: It is complex and only partially elucidated
It is fully explained by one gene
It is unrelated to insulin resistance
It is caused only by infection
questions.explanation: The exact mechanisms are under investigation, and the pathophysiology is complex and partially understood.

questions.questionText: Which cluster best reflects insulin-resistance-related metabolic syndrome?
options: Central obesity, impaired fasting glucose, high TG, low HDL
Low BMI, low glucose, high HDL, low TG
Underweight, hypotension, low TG, high HDL
Low waist circumference, low BP, low glucose
questions.explanation: Central obesity, impaired fasting glucose, hypertriglyceridemia, and low HDL fit the metabolic syndrome profile.

questions.questionText: Hyperuricemia in metabolic syndrome is:
options: One of the five main diagnostic criteria
A sign of vitamin deficiency only
questions.explanation: Hyperuricemia is listed as an associated condition, not one of the five main diagnostic criteria.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is NOT one of the five main criteria?
questions.explanation: Low calcium is not listed as a criterion. The criteria include BMI/central obesity, TG, HDL, BP, and fasting glucose.

questions.questionText: The prevalence of metabolic syndrome in the USA is estimated in some studies as approximately:
questions.explanation: The file states that some studies estimate prevalence in the USA at about 34% of the adult population.

questions.questionText: A male patient has BMI 32 kg/m², triglycerides 170 mg/dL, HDL 38 mg/dL, BP 124/80 mmHg, and fasting glucose 92 mg/dL. Does he meet metabolic syndrome criteria?
options: No, he has only two criteria
No, all five criteria are required
Yes, because BMI alone is enough
questions.explanation: He has BMI >30, TG >150, and HDL <40. That makes three criteria, enough for metabolic syndrome.

questions.questionText: A female patient has BMI 28 kg/m², TG 160 mg/dL, HDL 45 mg/dL, BP 138/86 mmHg, and fasting glucose 105 mg/dL. Does she meet criteria?
options: No, BMI must be >30 and is required
No, HDL is normal for females
questions.explanation: TG >150, HDL <50 in female, BP >130/85, and FPG >100 are present. She meets at least three criteria even though BMI is not >30.

questions.questionText: A male patient has BMI 29 kg/m², TG 140 mg/dL, HDL 42 mg/dL, BP 128/84 mmHg, and fasting glucose 98 mg/dL. Which interpretation is correct?
options: Does not meet metabolic syndrome criteria
Meets criteria due to BMI alone
Meets criteria due to HDL only
questions.explanation: None of the listed values cross the diagnostic thresholds, so he does not meet criteria.

questions.questionText: A female patient has BMI 31 kg/m², TG 145 mg/dL, HDL 48 mg/dL, BP 120/80 mmHg, and fasting glucose 90 mg/dL. Does she meet metabolic syndrome criteria?
options: No, only BMI and low HDL are present
Yes, all five criteria are present
questions.explanation: BMI >30 is one criterion, and HDL <50 in females is another. TG is not >150, BP is not raised, and glucose is not >100, so only two criteria are present.

questions.questionText: A male patient is on treatment for hypertension and has TG 180 mg/dL, HDL 45 mg/dL, BMI 27 kg/m², and fasting glucose 95 mg/dL. Does he meet criteria?
options: No, only two criteria are present
Yes, treatment for hypertension alone is enough
questions.explanation: He has TG >150 and treatment for hypertension. HDL 45 is not low for a male because the cutoff is <40. He has only two criteria.

questions.questionText: A male patient has diagnosed type 2 diabetes, BP 132/84 mmHg, TG 151 mg/dL, HDL 50 mg/dL, and BMI 26 kg/m². Does he meet metabolic syndrome criteria?
options: Yes, diabetes/FPG criterion + BP + TG = three criteria
questions.explanation: Previously diagnosed type 2 diabetes counts for the glucose criterion. BP systolic >130 and TG >150 add two more criteria.

questions.questionText: A female patient has HDL 49 mg/dL. Which interpretation is correct?
options: It meets the reduced HDL criterion for females
It is normal because only <40 counts in females
questions.explanation: For females, reduced HDL is <50 mg/dL, so 49 mg/dL meets the criterion.

questions.questionText: A male patient has HDL 39 mg/dL. Which interpretation is correct?
options: It meets the reduced HDL criterion for males
HDL is not part of metabolic syndrome
questions.explanation: In males, reduced HDL is <40 mg/dL.

questions.questionText: A patient has BP 128/88 mmHg. Does this meet the raised BP criterion?
options: Yes, because diastolic BP is >85 mmHg
No, systolic must be >130 and diastolic must also be >85
No, BP is normal by the file criteria
questions.explanation: The criterion is systolic >130 OR diastolic >85. A diastolic pressure of 88 meets the criterion.

questions.questionText: A patient has BP 132/82 mmHg. Does this meet the raised BP criterion?
options: Yes, because systolic BP is >130 mmHg
No, both systolic and diastolic must be raised
questions.explanation: Systolic BP >130 is enough to meet the criterion.

questions.questionText: A patient has fasting plasma glucose of 101 mg/dL. Which interpretation is correct?
options: It meets the raised fasting glucose criterion
It only counts if TG is normal
questions.explanation: Raised fasting plasma glucose is defined as >100 mg/dL.

questions.questionText: A sedentary older obese patient with insulin resistance and central adiposity develops high TG and low HDL. Which mechanism-level statement best fits the file?
options: Metabolic syndrome reflects disordered energy utilization/storage and is linked to insulin resistance
Metabolic syndrome is caused only by acute infection
Central obesity is unrelated to metabolic risk
questions.explanation: The file links metabolic syndrome to disturbed energy utilization/storage, obesity, sedentary behavior, and insulin resistance.

questions.questionText: A patient with central obesity, acanthosis nigricans, impaired fasting glucose, and low HDL is being evaluated. Which associated mechanism is most likely?
questions.explanation: Acanthosis nigricans and impaired fasting glucose are commonly associated with insulin resistance and metabolic syndrome.

questions.questionText: Which patient most likely has metabolic syndrome?
options: BMI 33, TG 180, HDL 35 male, BP 135/90, FPG 110
BMI 26, TG 120, HDL 55 female, BP 118/78, FPG 90
BMI 24, TG 100, HDL 50 male, BP 120/80, FPG 95
questions.explanation: Patient A meets all five criteria: BMI >30, TG >150, low HDL for male, raised BP, and FPG >100.

questions.questionText: A clinician sees an obese patient with fatty liver, hypertension, high TG, and prediabetes. Which conclusion is most appropriate?
options: The patient should be evaluated for metabolic syndrome
questions.explanation: Fatty liver is an associated condition, while hypertension, high TG, and prediabetes are core features of metabolic syndrome.
